title: Insurance Agencies and Brokerages

evidence_facts.title: Two-Thirds of Independent Agents Plan to Increase AI Use This Year
evidence_facts.quote: 8% – AI is embedded in daily workflows.

scope.population: Independent insurance agency members of various sizes responding to the Big I national email survey
scope.industry_mismatch: Independent agencies are a subset of NAICS 524210 and the member survey does not cover every firm in the code.

evidence_facts.title: Two-Thirds of Independent Agents Plan to Increase AI Use This Year

scope.population: Independent insurance agency members of various sizes responding to the Big I national email survey
scope.industry_mismatch: Independent agencies are a subset of NAICS 524210 and the member survey does not cover every firm in the code.

evidence_facts.title: Two-Thirds of Independent Agents Plan to Increase AI Use This Year

scope.population: Independent insurance agency members of various sizes responding to the Big I national email survey
scope.unit: percent of respondents naming an expected benefit
scope.industry_mismatch: Independent agencies are a subset of NAICS 524210 and an expected benefit is not a measured labor reduction.

evidence_facts.title: Producer Licensing
evidence_facts.quote: People who wish to sell, solicit or negotiate insurance in the United States must be licensed as a "producer".

scope.population: People who sell, solicit, or negotiate insurance
scope.industry_mismatch: The requirement concerns activities and people rather than firms classified in NAICS 524210.

evidence_facts.title: North American Agent & Broker Merger & Acquisition Update, 1st Quarter 2026
evidence_facts.quote: That leaves 38% (53) that did four or more deals ("Active Buyers").

scope.population: Distinct buyers in North American agent and broker M&A over the past eight quarters
scope.unit: buyers with four or more reported deals
scope.period: past eight quarters reported in the Q1 2026 update
scope.industry_mismatch: Agent and broker M&A can include businesses outside US NAICS 524210, including wholesale and other insurance-distribution businesses.

evidence_facts.title: North American Agent & Broker 2025 Year-end Merger & Acquisition Report
evidence_facts.quote: By some estimates there are over 30,000 independent agencies under $1.25M in revenue, the vast majority with no ability to perpetuate, so expect consolidation to continue with deal flow likely to remain at current levels.

scope.population: Independent agencies under $1.25 million in revenue
scope.unit: estimated agencies and stated ability to perpetuate
scope.industry_mismatch: Independent agencies are narrower than NAICS 524210 and the source does not identify NAICS classifications.

evidence_facts.title: How to Calculate the Value of an Insurance Agency
evidence_facts.quote: Firms in the insurance brokerage market typically transact at EBITDA multiples ranging from the mid-single digits to the mid-teens.

scope.population: Firms in the insurance brokerage market
scope.industry_mismatch: The source covers brokerage-market firms of unspecified size and characteristics, while the target band is a constructed US NAICS 524210 EBITDA band.

evidence_facts.title: How to Choose an Insurance Agent
evidence_facts.quote: Independent and captive agents represent insurance companies and receive a commission from the insurance company for the sale of its policies.

scope.industry_mismatch: The consumer guide describes agents generally and does not separately measure all NAICS 524210 agency and brokerage revenue streams.

evidence_facts.title: Big I and Future One Release 2024 Agency Universe Findings
evidence_facts.quote: Mergers & acquisitions activity and perpetuation challenges continue to impact the agency channel with 1 in 3 agencies expecting an ownership change in the next five years.

scope.population: Independent property and casualty agencies and brokers in the 2024 Agency Universe Study
scope.unit: share of agencies expecting an ownership change
scope.industry_mismatch: Independent property and casualty agencies are narrower than NAICS 524210, which also contains other insurance distribution businesses.

evidence_facts.title: Big I Releases 2026 Market Share Report
evidence_facts.quote: Independent agencies wrote 87.7% of commercial lines written premiums, consistent with 87.9% in 2024.

scope.industry_mismatch: Commercial-lines independent-agency distribution is a market channel, not the complete NAICS 524210 firm population.

evidence_facts.title: How to Choose an Insurance Agent
evidence_facts.quote: You can do business directly with many companies by purchasing coverage online. These policies may be less expensive since the company doesn't have to pay the agent's commission.

scope.unit: available purchase channel and stated cost mechanism
scope.industry_mismatch: The consumer guide is not a measured estimate of NAICS 524210 substitution or pricing effects.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 524210 ($103,879,237,202) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 524210 ($207,109,590,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Insurance Claims and Policy Processing Clerks
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists

n_band.derivation: margin 0.2357 (margins.json[524]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Insurance (General) EBITDA/Sales 23.57%); revenue/employee $256,440 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 524210); class <5: 91,066 firms, avg revenue/firm $323,793 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $76,318 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20,081 firms, avg revenue/firm $929,879 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $219,173 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 5,672 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,313,385 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $545,265 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,689 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,126,954 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,915,523 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 547 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,097,678 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,451,023 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 379 firms, avg revenue/firm $269,239,583 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,459,770 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,236

scope_mismatches: The AI survey is an independent-agency member survey rather than a task-level census of every NAICS 524210 occupation.
The licensing requirement concerns selling, soliciting and negotiating activities, not the automatable share of each SOC.
No source measures the residual occupation mix or task exposure by SOC.
bridge_steps: Use the reported embedded and limited AI use and operational-efficiency motive as evidence that automation is already relevant to agency workflow.
Separate clerical and processing tasks from licensed sales, advice and complex exception handling using the activity boundary in F4.
Apply task-level fractions to the supplied SOC labels without changing their wage shares or labels.
Assign a separate mixed-work estimate to the required residual wage share.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select within-role fractions that weight routine document and data workflow more heavily than licensed advice, relationship work and exception handling.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: AI can assist quoting, application intake, comparisons and proposal drafting, while licensed advice, relationship selling and complex placement remain human work.
Routine certificates, endorsements, policy checks and correspondence are workflow candidates, but service escalation and client advocacy remain human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Insurance Claims and Policy Processing Clerks
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Policy-processing and claims-support document and data workflows are highly automatable, subject to carrier-system integration and exception handling.
Reporting, workflow management and analysis can be AI-assisted, but leadership, carrier negotiation and hiring are not fully replaceable.
Routine desk review can be assisted, whereas field work, negotiation and disputed claims require human judgment.
General clerical intake, scheduling and document handling are workflow-heavy, with exceptions retained for people.
Risk-file assembly and triage can be automated, but delegated underwriting authority and appetite judgment retain human accountability.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, quality monitoring and reporting can be AI-assisted, but supervising staff and exception management remain human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Administrative communication, record preparation and scheduling are highly automatable aside from exceptions and executive coordination.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Campaign drafting, lead research and basic content production are automatable, while positioning and compliance review remain human.
The unlisted wage share likely combines service, administration and specialist work; a middle estimate recognizes mixed exposure without relabeling occupations.

pi_dist.rationale: Insurer-paid commissions and a large commercial-lines channel support retention of some operating savings, but carrier control, direct channels and fee-based brokerage work create material pass-through risk.

scope_mismatches: F8 and F11 are consumer-oriented descriptions rather than a financial study of NAICS 524210 pricing.
F9 and F10 cover independent property and casualty agencies, narrower than the full NAICS code.
None of the sources measures the fraction of AI savings retained by a target-band operator.
bridge_steps: Treat insurer-paid commissions in F8 as evidence that core agency revenue is not directly billed hourly to clients.
Use F10 as evidence of a presently important commercial channel and F11 as evidence of a direct-channel alternative.
Use F9's carrier and ownership-change pressure as background for possible bargaining and pass-through leakage.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.65 to reflect meaningful but incomplete operator retention of AI savings, with carrier repricing and direct or fee-based alternatives preventing a near-one distribution score.

pi_moat.rationale: Licensing and commercial-market presence protect part of the channel, but the same regulator notes many insurers sell direct online, so the moat is meaningful rather than durable by statute.

scope_mismatches: F4 describes a licensing rule for people and activities, not a firm-level competitive moat.
F10 is commercial-lines market share for the independent channel rather than a measure of target-firm defensibility.
F11 describes consumer options and does not quantify substitution across agency subsegments.
bridge_steps: Treat the producer-license requirement as a boundary on unlicensed selling, solicitation and negotiation.
Treat commercial-channel share as evidence of current distribution relevance.
Offset both with direct online availability and the stated commission cost mechanism in F11.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.45 because licensing and complex commercial distribution provide partial protection, while carrier direct distribution remains a credible substitute.

t50_years.rationale: Only 8% report embedded daily AI use and 22% limited-area use, while efficiency is the leading expected benefit; reaching material use by half of firms requires a forecast beyond the survey.

scope_mismatches: The survey population is independent-agency members rather than all US NAICS 524210 firms.
The response categories do not define the pipeline's threshold of material use in automatable functions.
The source is cross-sectional and does not provide a future adoption curve.
bridge_steps: Use F1 and F2 to establish that daily embedded use is low and limited-area use is materially higher.
Use F3 to establish a reported efficiency motive for adoption.
Do not extrapolate the survey mechanically because it supplies no time-series growth rate or target-population adoption threshold.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Forecast five years from the run date for 50% material firm adoption, recognizing integration, data and compliance constraints.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: The survey reports 8% embedded daily use and 22% limited-area use; the selected rate discounts limited experimentation and maps a member survey to material use across the code.

scope_mismatches: Independent-agency members are not the complete US NAICS 524210 firm population.
The reported daily and limited-use categories are not defined as material use in automatable functions.
The source reports respondent categories, not firm-weighted adoption.
bridge_steps: Use F1 as the directly embedded-use lower anchor.
Use F2 as evidence of broader but potentially incomplete use.
Discount the limited-use category because it may not meet the material-use definition and because the population differs from the target.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.25 as a judgmental current material-adoption rate between embedded daily use and the combined reported use categories.

historical_analogs.value: No quantified historical analog was found in the permitted live research. The current survey's split between embedded daily and limited-area AI use is used only as a staged-adoption context, not as a causal historical analogue.
historical_analogs.rationale: The available evidence is cross-sectional and cannot establish the speed or eventual depth of prior technology diffusion.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: No numerical analogue; adoption timing remains judgmental.

scope_mismatches: The cited source is a current cross-section, not a historical technology-adoption study.
It covers independent-agency survey respondents rather than all NAICS 524210 firms.
bridge_steps: Use the two current AI-use categories only to describe the present staged-adoption context.
Do not convert the categories into a historical rate-of-change estimate.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Characterize the absence of a usable historical analogue explicitly rather than asserting an unsupported comparison.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: Ownership-change expectations and the report's perpetuation concern indicate succession pressure, but neither source reports the share of owners aged 60 or older.

scope_mismatches: F6 identifies small independent agencies and does not report owner ages or geography.
F9 covers independent property and casualty agencies rather than all NAICS 524210 firms and reports expected ownership change, not age.
Neither source measures the target-band owner-age distribution.
bridge_steps: Use F9's one-in-three ownership-change expectation as a succession-pressure indicator.
Use F6's stated lack of ability to perpetuate among many small agencies as a second succession-pressure indicator.
Do not equate either indicator with age directly.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.35 as a cautious owner-60-plus estimate consistent with documented succession pressure but not claimed as an observed age statistic.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: The cited year-end M&A report directly states that the vast majority of the identified small independent agencies have no ability to perpetuate.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true for the cited small-independent-agency population; applicability to the full NAICS code is limited

active_consolidators.rationale: OPTIS explicitly labels the 53 buyers that completed four or more deals over the reported eight-quarter period as active buyers.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 53 for the cited North American, eight-quarter definition

buy_mult.rationale: The cited brokerage-market range is mid-single digits to mid-teens; 8x is a judgmental entry point for a conventional target-band agency after owner-compensation normalization, which the source does not supply.

scope_mismatches: F7 is a 2022 brokerage-market range with geography, target size, deal date and owner-compensation normalization not reported.
The target is a constructed US $1-10 million EBITDA band, not the source's unspecified firm population.
bridge_steps: Use F7 only as the broad observed market-multiple range.
Position a conventional target below the high-growth or large-firm end described by the source.
Require market-rate owner compensation conceptually, while recognizing the source does not provide a normalization calculation.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 8x as an estimated entry multiple within the cited range rather than claim it is an observed transaction multiple.

exit_mult.rationale: The cited brokerage-market range is broad and shows larger, high-growth, profitable firms can command higher multiples; 10x assumes only modest platform improvement and is not an observed exit quote.

scope_mismatches: F7 is a 2022 brokerage-market range with geography, exit-buyer population and owner-compensation normalization not reported.
The source does not measure a target-to-platform multiple spread for the constructed US target band.
bridge_steps: Use F7 as the broad observed market-multiple range.
Apply the source's stated size, growth and profitability characteristics as qualitative conditions rather than as a quantified platform uplift.
Do not treat deal activity as exit-multiple evidence.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 10x as a conservative estimated exit multiple within the cited range, leaving a modest spread over the estimated entry multiple.

pricing_structure.value: Predominantly insurer-paid commission revenue for agented policies, with some broker-fee work; carrier-set compensation and direct online alternatives limit how completely an agency can retain AI savings.
pricing_structure.rationale: NAIC states agents receive insurer-paid commissions and that direct online policies may be cheaper because no agent commission is paid; mapping that description to the complete code and retained savings requires judgment.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Commission-led core model with variable fee and carrier-repricing exposure by subsegment

scope_mismatches: Both sources are consumer guides rather than a revenue census for NAICS 524210 firms.
Neither source quantifies fee revenue, carrier commission schedules or the retention of AI savings.
bridge_steps: Use F8 as evidence of insurer-paid agent commissions.
Use F11 as evidence that direct distribution can remove the agent commission from policy cost.
Map these general compensation descriptions to the dominant independent-agency business model while retaining an explicit fee and carrier-repricing caveat.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Describe the pricing structure as commission-led rather than assert a fixed commission percentage or a full pass-through rate.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role estimate implies substantial addressable workflow, while a five-year t50 and the low current embedded-use rate keep near-term adoption slower than the eventual labor opportunity. Python supplies uplift_pp.

terminal_value.justification: AI can reduce service and processing cost without automatically replacing the commissioned product, and current commercial-lines channel share plus licensing support paid intermediation. However, direct online distribution can remove the agent commission and creates a product-replacement path, especially for simpler risks.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V and A use AI adoption, task and licensing evidence only; the M&A buyer evidence is used only for B and the valuation-range evidence only for M.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Insurer-paid commissions support partial margin retention, but carrier control and direct channels reduce both pi_dist and pi_moat below a full cost-savings capture assumption.

dataset_mismatch.description: No additional dataset mismatch was identified beyond the deterministic dataset's stated 5-digit role-mix and wage-share limitations.

subsegments.name: Independent retail property and casualty agencies and brokers
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Selected as dominant because the available Agency Universe and Market Share evidence concerns this independent P&C channel; the source does not provide an NAICS-wide revenue split.

subsegments.name: Employee-benefits and life and health agencies
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because the NAICS title is broader than P&C; exact revenue rank is a judgment because the permitted sources do not report a full code revenue mix.

subsegments.name: Managing general agencies, managing general underwriters and wholesale broker operations
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because delegated-underwriting and wholesale operations have different task mixes and valuation economics; their share of NAICS 524210 revenue is not reported in the cited sources.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Independent retail property and casualty agencies and brokers
market_boundary.selection_basis: Score the independent retail P&C agency and broker subsegment because the live adoption, ownership, market-share and channel evidence is specific to it; disclose the unmeasured mix rather than apply those results to every subsegment.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A equals 10 divided by t50, so adoption timing materially changes A.
The estimate moves V_raw through the labor-share multiplication and can move V around its cap.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: Entry 6x-11x and exit 7x-13x EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: The entry-exit spread directly determines M and could compress to zero.

reviewer_note: All judgmental population mappings, task fractions, adoption timing, owner-age inference, pricing capture and target-to-platform multiple choices are explicitly classified as estimates.

V.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: V_raw = labor_share × ai_replaceable_share = 0.5348 × 0.4824 = 0.25798752; V = 10 × min(1, V_raw / 0.25) = 10

C.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: C = 10 × pi_dist × pi_moat = 10 × 0.65 × 0.45 = 2.925

A.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: A = 10 when t50_years = 0, otherwise min(10, 10 / t50_years); t50_years = 5, A = 2

M.arithmetic: Python finalizer v3.1.1: M = clamp(4 × (exit_mult − buy_mult) / buy_mult, 0, 10) = 1
